- Global infrastructure market: $5.18 trillion by 2030
- Companies in the Webuild supply chain: 17,500
- Safety training hours: 3 million in the 2022-2025 three-year period

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). If the collaboration between institutions and businesses becomes a reality, cross-border workers in Ticino and Italian professionals operating in the canton must be ready to seize new opportunities. Here’s what to do step by step to avoid being caught off guard.

### Step 1: Check ongoing infrastructure projects

The first construction sites that could benefit from Webuild’s collaborative approach are those already in the planning or early stages. For cross-border workers, it’s essential to:

- Consult the Ticino Department of Education and Employment (DFE) website ([nav:jobs](nav:jobs)) to monitor tenders and job opportunities in key sectors (construction, transport, energy).
- Check announcements from Webuild and the other 17,500 companies in the supply chain, available on their institutional websites or specialized platforms like [nav:companies](nav:companies).
- Sign up for newsletters from the Italian-Swiss Chamber of Commerce or the Ticino Order of Engineers to receive real-time updates.

### Step 2: Update your skill set

Workplace safety and digitalization are two pillars of the Webuild model. For cross-border workers, this means:

- Taking part in safety training courses for construction sites, such as those organized by SUPSI or EOC. Some courses are already recognized in Lombardy and Ticino.
- Learning digital tools used in major infrastructure projects, such as BIM (Building Information Modeling) or construction management platforms.
- Obtaining specific certifications, for example, to work in high-risk environments or operate specialized machinery. Webuild offers internal courses that, in some cases, may also be extended to external professionals.

> ⚠️ Warning: Some skills, such as workplace safety according to Swiss regulations (SUVA), are mandatory for working on construction sites in Ticino. Always verify the requirements before applying.

### Step 3: Take part in networking initiatives

The collaboration between institutions and businesses also relies on sharing expertise. Cross-border workers can contribute actively by participating in:

- Technical roundtables organized by the DFE or Lombardy Region, where cross-border infrastructure projects are discussed.
- Sector-specific events, such as those promoted by Museimpresa or the Politecnico di Milano SUPSI, which bring together businesses, institutions, and professionals.
- Working groups on dual education, where training pathways shared between Ticino and Lombardy are designed.

### Step 4: Monitor tax incentives and benefits

To attract investments and talent, the Canton of Ticino and the Lombardy Region may introduce new tax benefits or training grants. Keep an eye on:

- The Ticino DFE website for any regional or cantonal tenders.
- Communications from SUPSI and the Università della Svizzera Italiana (USI), which often promote training projects with public funding.
- Bilateral agreements between Switzerland and Italy, which may include tax relief for those working in key sectors.

### Step 5: Prepare for regulatory changes

The collaboration between institutions and businesses could lead to new regulations in areas such as:
- Cross-border public procurement: Simplified procedures for shared projects.
- Recognition of skills: Greater flexibility in validating professional qualifications between Ticino and Lombardy.
- Workplace safety: Alignment of standards between the two countries.

[object Object]

*Source: [varesenoi.it](https://www.varesenoi.it/2026/04/15/leggi-notizia/articolo/webuild-salini-serve-collaborazione-istituzioni-e-imprese-per-paese-competitivo.html)*

item.title: Iran dashes Italy's hopes of World Cup 2026 reprieve
item.description: Iranian national team head coach Amir Ghalenoei has ruled out the possibility of his side withdrawing from the tournament: 'We will be there.' FIFA is now exploring alternative options to fill any potential gaps in the competition.
item.content:encoded:
The Italian national football team is in a precarious position regarding qualification for the 2026 World Cup in Canada, Mexico, and the United States. Following their defeat in the European play-off final against Bosnia, the Azzurri had hoped for a reprieve thanks to a potential withdrawal by Iran. That hope has now been drastically diminished by statements from Iranian head coach Amir Ghalenoei, who ruled out any possibility of skipping the tournament. 

### The Iranian turning point
With just weeks to go before the start of the tournament’s final phase, FIFA had considered the possibility of a reprieve for Italy if Iran had withdrawn. The reasoning? The ongoing conflict between Iran and the United States, which are hosting the World Cup alongside Canada and Mexico. However, Ghalenoei’s words have dashed this hypothesis: 'There is no reason not to participate in the World Cup. God willing, we will do it,' he told the German news agency Dpa. 

The final decision, though, does not lie with the coach but with the Iranian government, which has previously expressed its opposition to playing in the United States. Iran had shown willingness to participate only if its group-stage matches were moved to Mexico, a condition FIFA has so far rejected. Italy’s fate now hinges solely on political choices in Tehran and developments in the Middle East conflict. 

### FIFA’s rules and alternative scenarios
Under FIFA regulations (Article 6.7), if a national team withdraws, the international body may select a replacement 'at its sole discretion.' Italy, currently ranked 12th in the FIFA rankings, would stand a good chance of being among the candidates, though at present this remains a remote possibility. 

If Iran were to indeed pull out of the World Cup, FIFA could opt for a reprieve among the teams excluded just before the tournament. One scenario under consideration is a four-team intercontinental playoff, featuring two European sides (likely Italy and Denmark) and two Asian teams. The teams would be split into two semi-finals, with the winners facing off in a single final. This approach, inspired by a recent precedent in the first edition of the FIFA Club World Cup, could realistically pave the way for Italy’s return—but only in the event of Iran’s absence. For Ticino cross-border workers who follow Italian football, the situation now brings fresh uncertainty. The hope of seeing Italy at the 2026 World Cup – dashed by the bitter playoff loss to Bosnia – grows fainter with Iran’s latest statements. But what would it actually mean for daily life for those who commute between Italy and Ticino? 

### Before vs after: the impact on the sports calendar
Until recently, Ticino cross-border workers could look forward to a richer calendar during the World Cup period. Italy’s qualification would have meant matches at the **Giuseppe Meazza Stadium in Milan**, easily accessible from Ticino via direct connections at the Brogeda or Chiasso border crossings. Additionally, Italy’s bank holidays and TV broadcasts could have influenced work habits, with possible requests for time off or reduced shifts from Ticino employers. 

Now, however, Italy’s absence from the World Cup seems almost certain. This means cross-border commuters will have to settle for following other competitions or simply cheer for other European teams, such as Denmark, which could still feature in a potential playoff. 

### Possible FIFA scenarios
If FIFA decides to organize a four-team intercontinental playoff, Italy would be among the favorites for inclusion thanks to its FIFA ranking. However, the timeline would be tight: the World Cup finals begin on June 11, 2026, meaning any playoffs would have to take place between May and June – precisely when many cross-border workers are on summer holidays. This could create logistical challenges for both fans and employers. 

Alternatively, FIFA could opt to fill the vacant spot quickly by assigning it to an Asian team, such as the United Arab Emirates – already eliminated by Iraq in the qualification path. In this case, Italy would remain out, and cross-border commuters would have to adjust to following tournaments with less centrality in European sporting debates. 

> Italy’s FIFA ranking is an advantage, but it’s no guarantee. FIFA could decide differently, especially if a rapid replacement is needed.

📊💡⚠️ Key takeaways for Ticino cross-border workers:
- No World Cup matches in Milan means fewer travel opportunities for fans.
- Bank holidays and TV broadcasts tied to Italy’s campaign would no longer impact work schedules.
- Alternative tournaments may lack the same cultural and media resonance in Ticino.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers from Ticino who want to stay updated on the situation and assess the practical implications, here's a step-by-step guide to monitor developments and prepare for any changes.

### Step 1: Follow official updates
FIFA will publish the final decisions regarding national team participation in the 2026 World Cup on its official page ([fifa.com](nav:calendar)). For those working between Italy and Ticino, it is advisable to check qualification news at least once a week, especially between May and June 2026, when playoffs or last-minute changes could be announced.

### Step 2: Assess the impact on free time
If Italy were to qualify for the World Cup through playoffs, cross-border workers might need to arrange time off or special leave to attend matches. In Ticino, the public holidays during the tournament (e.g., June 15, 2026, which could be a match day) are not recognized as cantonal holidays, so prior agreement with employers will be necessary.

### Step 3: Plan alternatives for watching football on TV
In the event of Italy's exclusion, cross-border workers could opt to follow other competitions, such as the Champions League or the Italian league, which remain regularly scheduled. For those who prefer live football, tickets for Swiss league matches (e.g., at Stadio Cornaredo in Lugano) are a valid and easily accessible alternative.

### Step 4: Keep an eye on travel offers
If FIFA were to organize an intercontinental playoff, matches could take place in the United States. For cross-border workers, this would mean evaluating costs and flight availability from Milan Malpensa or Lugano-Agno, with possible layovers in Zurich or Geneva. An option to consider in advance, especially for those already planning summer trips.

### Step 5: Use Frontaliere Ticino's tools
For those looking to explore how this situation could impact their salary or holiday planning, our [salary calculator](nav:calculator) allows you to simulate different scenarios based on working hours and public holidays. Additionally, for those interested in following football in Ticino, our [sports event guide](nav:calendar) provides an updated calendar of local matches and fan initiatives.

*Source: [varesenoi.it](https://www.varesenoi.it/2026/04/15/leggi-notizia/articolo/italia-e-ripescaggio-ai-mondiali-iran-spegne-le-speranze-il-ct-ci-saremo.html)*

item.title: Ticino restaurateur catches thief by surprise: 35-year-old man arrested
item.description: A restaurateur in Mendrisio caught a 35-year-old man red-handed while he was stealing in the kitchen. The intruder, of Moroccan nationality, was detained and handed over to the cantonal police.
item.content:encoded:
Last night, around 9:30 PM, a Ticinese restaurateur made an unexpected discovery during their shift in Mendrisio. While inside the restaurant, they noticed suspicious movement near the kitchen area. Upon closer inspection, they were surprised to find a 35-year-old man of Moroccan nationality rummaging through the pantry shelves. According to the cantonal police, the intruder did not merely observe: they had actually taken some items, likely food or utensils, before being caught.

Without hesitation, the restaurateur detained the man and held them until law enforcement arrived. According to initial reports, the individual did not resist but attempted to justify their actions with an unconvincing excuse. The Mendrisio cantonal police promptly intervened and arrested the 35-year-old for breaking and entering and theft.

The investigation is now in the hands of the prosecutors, who will determine whether this was an isolated incident or if accomplices were involved. The restaurateur, who chose to remain anonymous, stated they acted out of a sense of duty and to protect their livelihood. The incident has surprised the local community, particularly due to how quickly the situation was resolved, avoiding more serious consequences.

### The regulatory context in Ticino

In Ticino, breaking and entering is governed by the Swiss Criminal Code, specifically Article 186, which imposes custodial sentences of up to three years for anyone unlawfully entering a private residence or premises. In this case, the charges could be aggravated if it is confirmed that the intruder intended to steal valuable items, not just food. The cantonal police have already initiated procedures to verify any prior criminal record of the 35-year-old, who has lived in Ticino for several years.

### Reactions from the local community

The news quickly spread among residents of Mendrisio and surrounding municipalities, raising concerns among local shopkeepers. Many emphasized the importance of maintaining high vigilance, especially at a time when thefts and attempted break-ins appear to be on the rise. Some speculated that the incident might be linked to social hardship, though there is currently no concrete evidence to support this. The incident involving a restaurateur from Mendrisio and a 35-year-old man, arrested for burglary and theft, raises several points for reflection, particularly for those living and working in Ticino. Above all, it highlights the importance of prevention and individual responsibility: the restaurateur acted promptly, preventing the situation from escalating and demonstrating how even in everyday contexts, small actions can make a real difference.

### Implications for Ticino’s business owners

For small and medium-sized entrepreneurs in the Canton of Ticino, security remains one of their top concerns. According to data from the Ticino cantonal police, in 2023 there were over 1,200 reported cases of burglary and theft targeting commercial premises, a slight increase compared to previous years. Restaurants, in particular, are among the most frequent targets, often due to the presence of valuable food items and equipment.

In this context, many businesses are considering additional security measures, such as installing closed-circuit cameras, alarm systems, or hiring private security guards during night hours. However, these solutions come with significant costs that many entrepreneurs struggle to bear, especially in a period marked by inflation and rising operational expenses.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Before the Mendrisio incident, local business owners primarily relied on traditional security systems, such as window grates and reinforced doors. However, the case has shown that, in some instances, a human presence can make all the difference. Many restaurateurs are now considering hiring staff to monitor their premises even after closing hours, at least during the early hours of the night when the risk of break-ins is highest.

### Possible scenarios for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who operate commercial or restaurant businesses in Ticino, security is an even more delicate issue. Many of them spend part of their day in Italy and cannot constantly monitor their premises. In such cases, collaborating with neighbors or other local business owners can prove crucial. Some industry associations have already started initiatives to raise awareness among traders about the importance of prevention, offering free consultations on how to enhance the security of their establishments.

> 📊 **Key figures**: In 2023, Ticino’s cantonal police recorded over 1,200 cases of burglary and theft in commercial premises, with restaurants being a primary target.

> ⚠️ Food for thought: The Mendrisio incident underscores how a proactive approach—even in everyday situations—can prevent escalation. Would your business benefit from additional security measures?

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). If you are responsible for the security of your commercial premises in Ticino, here is a step-by-step guide to assess and improve your protection measures, inspired by best practices that have emerged following incidents such as the one in Mendrisio.

### Step 1: Risk assessment

First, conduct a risk assessment specific to your premises. Consider factors such as the location (city centre, outskirts, commercial area), opening and closing hours, and the value of the goods present. For example, in a restaurant, food and utensils may be an attractive target for thieves.

What to do:
- Identify the weak points of your premises (windows, secondary doors, rear entrances).
- Check if your business is already equipped with grilles, security locks, or alarm systems.
- Consult the Mendrisio cantonal police or your local municipality for tailored advice on preventive measures.

### Step 2: Implementing basic security measures

Even without investing in advanced technologies, you can enhance the security of your premises with simple and cost-effective solutions. Here are some options:

- **Security locks**: Ensure all doors and windows are fitted with certified locks, preferably with keys that cannot be easily duplicated.
- External lighting: Install motion-sensor lights in outdoor areas of the premises. This can deter night-time intrusions.
- Closed-circuit cameras: Even a single camera positioned strategically can act as a deterrent. Make sure it is visible and that recordings are stored for at least 30 days.
- Neighbourhood collaboration: Inform nearby residents or businesses about your closing hours and encourage them to report any suspicious activity.

### Step 3: Evaluating advanced solutions

If your budget allows, consider adopting more sophisticated systems, such as:

- Alarm systems connected to an operations centre: These systems can alert law enforcement in case of intrusion, reducing response times.
- Security guards: In some areas, you can arrange with private security agencies to patrol your premises during night hours.

⚠️ Important: Before installing any system, verify that it complies with Ticino’s privacy and surveillance regulations.

### Step 4: Staff training

If you have employees, ensure they are trained on how to act in case of an intrusion. Here are some tips:
- Teach them not to confront intruders directly but to call the police immediately on 117.
- Establish an emergency protocol with reference phone numbers and procedures to follow.
- Organise regular drills to simulate emergency situations.

### Step 5: Reporting and filing a complaint

If you experience a theft attempt or a breach of your premises, it is essential to file a report with the cantonal police. This helps authorities monitor crime trends and implement more effective preventive measures.

What to do:
- Contact the nearest police station or call 117.
- Provide a detailed description of the incident and, if possible, of the individuals involved.
- Preserve all evidence (photos, videos, testimonies) that could assist investigations.

### Useful resources

For further information and support, you can contact:
- Ticino cantonal police: [Official website](nav:traffic-history) to report incidents or request consultations.
- Professional associations: Many organisations, such as the Ticino Chamber of Commerce, offer security consultation services.
- Local municipalities: Some Ticino municipalities provide grants to finance the installation of security systems.

### Conclusion

The incident in Mendrisio serves as an important reminder: security is never guaranteed, even in an apparently peaceful context like Ticino. Adopting preventive measures, collaborating with local authorities, and training staff are key steps to protecting your business and livelihood. If you are unsure how to improve the security of your premises, do not hesitate to seek support from the competent institutions.

Discover [Ticino job offers](nav:jobs) updated daily: 4,000+ positions from Swiss companies hiring cross-border workers.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/wirt-uberrascht-einbrecher-im-restaurant-marokkaner-festgenommen/)*

item.title: Accident in Cassano Magnago: two Ticino cross-border workers injured
item.description: Two Ticino cross-border workers were hospitalized following a violent car collision in Cassano Magnago (Varese). No diverted traffic on A26 or A8.
item.content:encoded:
A violent head-on collision between two cars involved two Ticinese cross-border workers employed in Varese. The accident occurred early this morning, Wednesday, April 10, 2025, along the provincial road SP121 between Cassano Magnago and Gorla Minore, just a few kilometers from the A8 motorway exit (Milan-Varese).

According to local authorities, both drivers — residents of Ticino and regular commuters to Lombardy — were rescued at the scene by firefighters and emergency medical personnel from the 118 service. The two injured, a 38-year-old man and a 42-year-old woman, were transported in critical condition to Busto Arsizio Hospital, where they are currently hospitalized for treatment. No further details about their health status have been provided, but local sources indicate they are in critical condition.

### Accident dynamics

The Varese traffic police have reconstructed the collision: the two vehicles, one traveling north and the other south, collided head-on on a straight stretch of the SP121. The impact caused the complete crumpling of the front sections, requiring emergency tools to free the trapped drivers. Weather conditions at the time of the accident were normal, with no rain or fog, so no environmental factors are suspected to have contributed.

Rescuers reported the presence of a third vehicle marginally involved, a car parked on the roadside, which sustained minor damage. Authorities have opened a file for investigation, pending technical assessments to determine liability.

### Impact on local traffic

The accident caused the temporary closure of the SP121 in both directions, resulting in long queues toward Varese and Gallarate. Traffic officers diverted vehicles onto secondary roads, including SP140 and SP130, to ease congestion. According to initial assessments, traffic returned to normal after about two hours, but commuters heading to Lombardy in the coming hours are advised to exercise caution.

Authorities have urged drivers to exercise extreme caution, particularly on extra-urban stretches, where the average speed of Ticinese commuters often exceeds legal limits.

### Reactions from cross-border communities

The news quickly spread through social groups dedicated to Ticinese cross-border workers, with messages of solidarity and concern. Many users emphasized the importance of adhering to speed limits and maintaining focus during daily commutes.

Some commenters noted that the Cassano Magnago-Gallarate stretch is one of the busiest roads in the Varese province, with a daily flow of over 15,000 vehicles, many heading toward industrial hubs like Busto Arsizio and Legnano. The presence of trucks and heavy goods vehicles makes the area particularly hazardous in the event of accidents.

### Next steps

The Varese prosecutor's office has launched an investigation to determine the causes of the accident, with particular attention to speeding and compliance with traffic laws. The drivers involved, once discharged from the hospital, will be interviewed to reconstruct the collision dynamics.

Meanwhile, associations representing Ticinese cross-border workers have called on cantonal authorities to consider targeted safety interventions on border roads, such as the installation of speed bumps or illuminated signage in the most dangerous stretches. The Cassano Magnago incident raises questions about road safety for the roughly 68,000 cross-border workers from Ticino who cross the border into Lombardy every day. According to data from the Ticino Chamber of Commerce, over 70% of these workers commute by car, with an average travel time of 40-50 minutes between home and workplace.

### Specific risks for Ticino commuters

The route between Cassano Magnago and the Swiss border (Gaggiolo crossing) presents several critical points:

- **Speed limits**: The SP121 is a provincial road with a speed limit of 90 km/h, but many Ticino commuters—accustomed to Swiss highways (A2, A8) with limits of 120 km/h—often exceed the limit.
- Mixed traffic: The presence of heavy vehicles and private cars creates hazardous situations, particularly on straight sections where average speeds frequently exceed 80 km/h.
- Lack of direct highways: Unlike other border areas (e.g., Chiasso with the A9), the route to Varese does not have a direct highway, forcing commuters to travel on provincial and state roads with curves and pedestrian crossings.
- Weather conditions: Although today's incident occurred in stable weather, morning fog in autumn and winter drastically reduces visibility, increasing the risk of head-on collisions.

📊 Comparison with other border routes

| Route | Road type | Speed limit | Daily traffic | Accident risk |
|-------|-----------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| Chiasso (A2) → Milan | A9 highway | 130 km/h | ~20,000 | Medium |
| Stabio (A3) → Como | Cantonal road + SP | 80 km/h | ~12,000 | High |
| Cassano Magnago → Ticino | Provincial road | 90 km/h | ~15,000 | Very high |

*Source: Data processed from Ticino Chamber of Commerce and Varese Traffic Police*

### Emergency procedures for cross-border workers

What should Ticino workers do in case of an accident in Lombardy?

- Call 112 (European emergency number) or 118 for medical assistance.
- Report the incident to local authorities (Varese Traffic Police) by filling out the CID form (European Accident Statement) if possible.
- Contact your Swiss insurer within 24 hours to start the compensation process. Swiss mandatory third-party liability insurance (RCA) also covers accidents abroad, but it is essential to submit the documentation promptly.
- Inform your employer in Ticino, especially if the incident could cause delays or absences from work.
- Check your health coverage: The Ticino healthcare system (EOC) recognizes treatment received in Italy, but it is advisable to carry the European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) or private supplementary insurance.

💡 Practical tips to reduce risks

- Observe speed limits strictly, especially on the SP121.
- Maintain a safe distance from heavy vehicles and be extra cautious at pedestrian crossings.
- Use daytime running lights in low-visibility conditions.
- Plan your route in advance to avoid peak traffic times.
- Consider alternative transport options, such as trains or carpooling, if possible.

⚠️ Key takeaway

The Cassano Magnago incident highlights the urgent need for improved road safety measures for Ticino commuters. Authorities and employers should work together to raise awareness, enforce traffic rules, and explore infrastructure improvements to protect the thousands of workers who rely on this route daily.

## Key safety rules for cross-border commuters

1. Respect speed limits: Do not exceed 90 km/h on SP121 and reduce speed in foggy conditions or heavy traffic.
2. Maintain a safe distance: In case of traffic jams or sudden slowdowns, keep at least a 2-second gap from the vehicle ahead.
3. Avoid distractions: Using mobile phones, sat-navs, or engaging in conversations with passengers increases the risk of accidents.
4. Check tyres and brakes: Before setting off, especially after winter months, it is advisable to inspect tyre wear and the condition of the braking system.
5. Use alternative routes: In heavy traffic, consider taking SP140 (Gallarate-Varese) or SP130 (Busto Arsizio-Tradate), which are often less congested.

### Impact on insurance

Road accidents in Italy frequently involve cross-border workers from Ticino, many of whom are unaccustomed to Italian procedures. According to USTAT data, around 300 serious injury accidents involving cross-border commuters are recorded in Ticino each year, with an average of 2-3 deaths annually.

Swiss third-party liability insurance policies do cover accidents abroad, but claim settlement times can be longer than in Switzerland. It is therefore advisable to:

- Verify that the policy includes coverage for third-party liability and property damage.
- Always carry the CID form (in paper or digital format) to streamline insurance procedures.
- Contact your insurer promptly and provide all required information, including bank details for any compensation.

> 📊 Did you know?
> Swiss insurance policies for cross-border commuters may have specific clauses for accidents in Italy. Always check the fine print.

### Role of cantonal authorities

The Ticino Department of Finance and Economy (DFE) has launched a collaboration with the Lombardy Region to enhance road safety along border routes. Among the measures under discussion:

- Installation of speed bumps in the most dangerous sections.
- Improvement of lighting at intersections and curves.
- Awareness campaigns targeting cross-border commuters, with a focus on young drivers and new licence holders.
- Driver training on how to act in case of an accident on Italian territory.

These initiatives, however, require time and resources, while cross-border workers must daily face the risks of the Cassano Magnago-Gaggiolo route.

> 💡 Tip
> Before travelling, check real-time traffic updates on platforms like Ticino Mobilità or [Autostrade.it](nav:traffico_italia) to plan your journey efficiently.

> ⚠️ Warning
> Italian traffic police (Polizia Stradale) strictly enforce speed limits and other road rules. Non-compliance can result in hefty fines and points deducted from your licence.

## Useful tools for your case
To verify your within/over 20 km tax scenario, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [tax return guide](nav:tax-return). Are you a Ticino cross-border worker who commutes daily along SP121 between Cassano Magnago and the Swiss border? Here’s what you **must do immediately** after an accident, step by step.

### Step 1: Ensure safety and call for help

- Assess the severity: If there are injuries, call 112 (European emergency number) or 118 for medical assistance right away.
- Report your location: Provide authorities with the exact kilometer of the incident (e.g., SP121, km 5+200) to speed up response times.
- Do not move the vehicles: Unless they pose an immediate danger to traffic, leave the vehicles in their collision position to allow experts to reconstruct the accident.

### Step 2: Complete the CID form (European Accident Statement)

- Download the CID form digitally from your insurer’s website (e.g., AXA, Allianz, Generali) or keep a printed copy in your car.
- Fill in all fields, including:
 - Vehicle details (license plate, make, model).
 - Driver information (name, surname, address, driver’s license number).
 - Accident description (with a schematic drawing).
 - Witnesses (if any).
- Sign the form only if you agree with the other driver’s account. If you disagree, do not sign and leave it blank.

### Step 3: Contact your insurer

- Call your insurer’s helpline within 24 hours of the accident. Provide them with:
 - CID claim number (if completed).
 - Details of the other driver and their vehicle.
 - Photos of the damage and the accident scene.
 - Medical report (if you were injured).
- Submit the required documents: Many insurers accept paperwork via email or through a dedicated app. Keep all receipts (e.g., pharmacy, taxi fare to return home).

### Step 4: Notify your employer

- Inform your HR department or workplace safety officer, especially if the accident may cause delays or absences.
- Bring a medical certificate with you if you were injured, even for a few hours. In Ticino, employers must be notified of any work-related injuries, even if they occur abroad.

### Step 5: Submit the claim to INPS (if required)

If the accident results in temporary work disability, you may be entitled to daily benefits. In this case:

- Request a medical certificate from the Italian emergency room or your Ticino GP.
- Submit the documentation to INPS via their online portal ([inps.it](nav:jobs)) or at a local employment center (e.g., ACLI, CISL).
- Track your days off: INPS requires detailed certification of lost working days.

### Step 6: Assess vehicle damage

- Take your car to a trusted garage in Ticino or Lombardy for an appraisal. If you have comprehensive coverage, contact your insurer to authorize repairs.
- Compare quotes: In Lombardy, repair costs may be lower than in Switzerland, but always verify the garage is approved by your insurer.
- Claim compensation: If the other driver is at fault, their insurer should cover your vehicle’s damages. If not, your third-party liability (RCA) or comprehensive policy will step in.

### Step 7: Monitor your claim status

> ⚠️ Important reminder: Always keep copies of all documents, receipts, and communications with insurers, employers, and authorities. Delays in reporting or incomplete paperwork can complicate the claims process.

- Keep your insurer updated on the status of assessments and compensation.
- In case of delays, send a written communication (email or registered letter) to follow up on your claim.
- If the compensation is insufficient, you can file a complaint with the Italian Institute for the Supervision of Private Insurance (IVASS) or with FINMA in Switzerland, depending on the insurance company involved.

### Deadlines to remember

| Requirement | Maximum deadline | Required documentation |
|-------------|------------------|-------------------------|
| Report to the insurer | 24 hours | CID form, photographs, medical report (if injured) |
| Request for INPS allowance | 30 days from the incident | Medical certificate, employer’s statement |
| Filing a complaint with IVASS/FINMA | 6 months | Insurance documentation, correspondence with the company |

### Useful tools for cross-border workers

To best manage the situation after an incident, here are some resources:

- Digital CID form: [Download here](nav:permit-compare) (compatible with Swiss insurers).
- INPS guide for cross-border workers: [Visit the dedicated page](nav:jobs) for instructions on how to request the allowance.
- Compensation calculator: [Use our tool](nav:calculator) to estimate the value of vehicle damage based on make and model.
- List of authorized garages in Lombardy: [Find the nearest one to you](nav:car-transfer).

### What happens if you don’t report the incident?

- Risk of penalties: In Italy, failing to report an accident involving personal injury or property damage can result in fines of up to €3,000 and a driving licence suspension.

- Insurance issues: If you don’t report the incident promptly, your insurer may refuse to compensate for vehicle damage.

- Difficulty returning home: Without a medical certificate, you may face challenges justifying absences from work or delays in submitting documents to your employer.

📌 In summary: After an accident in Lombardy, act quickly: call for help, complete the CID form, contact your insurer, and notify your employer. Leave nothing to chance, especially if you’re a cross-border worker accustomed to navigating two different regulatory systems.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/cassano-magnago-varese-violenta-collisione-due-persone-in-ospedale/)*

item.title: Property purchases from abroad: Swiss government tightens rules
item.description: From 15 April to 15 July 2026, a public consultation on new restrictions for non-EU/EFTA foreigners. What’s changing for homes, chalets and investments.
item.content:encoded:
The Federal Council has launched a public consultation that will run until **15 July 2026** to amend the law on the purchase of real estate by foreign individuals, known as the Lex Koller. The stated aim is to tackle Switzerland’s housing shortage, a problem that also affects Ticino, where demand for residential properties remains high, particularly in urban centres like Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno.

### What the new draft law proposes
Under the proposal, citizens from third countries (outside the EU and EFTA) will need to obtain authorisation to purchase a primary residence in Switzerland. If they move abroad, they will be required to sell the property within two years. Currently, no such rule exists for non-EU foreigners buying a home as their primary residence.

For holiday homes and apartments in tourist residences, the Government suggests reducing the annual quotas allocated to the cantons. Additionally, the obligation to obtain authorisation will be reintroduced for sales of these properties between foreigners. At present, for example, if a German citizen sells a holiday apartment to a French citizen, the cantonal quota remains unaffected. Under the new rules, every such transaction will require authorisation.

### Commercial properties and financial investments
The tightening of rules also applies to commercial real estate. Foreigners will no longer be able to purchase such properties for rental or financial investment purposes; they will only be permitted to do so if they use them for their own business activities. This measure aims to curb real estate transactions that do not directly impact the residential rental market.

Another key change concerns the purchase of shares in real estate securities. The Federal Council wants to ban entirely the acquisition, by foreigners, of shares in publicly traded residential real estate companies or stakes in real estate funds and SICAVs. Currently, this type of investment is permitted without restrictions.

### Motivations and political context
These measures are part of a package of provisions decided by the Federal Council ahead of the June 2026 vote on the popular initiative *'No to a Switzerland of 10 million!'*. The initiative, backed by the Swiss People’s Party (SVP/UDC), links the housing crisis to uncontrolled immigration and population growth. The Government, however, believes the solution lies in stricter management of the real estate market rather than broad restrictions on the population.

### When will the new rules come into force?
At present, the text is in the public consultation phase and may undergo changes before final approval. It has not yet been specified when the new legislation will come into effect, but the Federal Council has indicated that the goal is to approve it by the end of 2026. The new restrictions on real estate purchases by foreigners will have a direct impact on **cross-border workers living in Ticino** who own properties abroad, as well as on those planning to invest in the Swiss real estate market. The measure introduces a series of practical complications that could discourage some purchases, particularly those linked to financial investments or second homes.

### Before vs. After: What Changes for Cross-Border Workers
Before the new rules:
- A non-EU citizen (for example, an Italian) could buy a home in Ticino as a primary residence without authorization, provided they actually lived there.
- Purchases of holiday homes or apartments in tourist residences were subject to cantonal quotas, but sales between foreigners did not require additional permits.
- Commercial properties could be purchased freely, even for rental or investment purposes.
- Buying shares in real estate funds or SICAVs was permitted without restrictions.

After the new rules:
- Primary residence: Mandatory authorization and resale within two years if moving abroad.
- Holiday homes and tourist apartments: Reduced quotas and mandatory authorization even for sales between foreigners.
- Commercial properties: Purchase for rental or financial investment is prohibited; only personal or business use is allowed.
- Real estate securities: Complete ban on foreigners purchasing shares in residential real estate companies or shares in real estate funds.

📊 Concrete Scenarios for Those Living in Ticino

> Case 1: Italian Cross-Border Worker Owning a Home in Ticino
> If a cross-border worker residing in Lombardy already owns a home in Ticino as their primary residence, they won’t need to take immediate action. However, they will need authorization if they wish to buy a second property. If they move abroad, they must sell the property within two years. The new rule does not apply retroactively, but it could influence future transactions.

> Case 2: Foreign Investor Looking to Buy an Apartment in Lugano
> A US or Chinese citizen planning to buy an apartment in Lugano as an investment will have to abandon the project, as purchasing commercial properties for rental purposes is now prohibited. If they instead want to buy a home for personal use, they must obtain authorization and prove they actually reside in Switzerland.

> Case 3: Owner of a Holiday Home in Ascona
> If a German citizen owns a holiday home in Ascona and wants to sell it to another foreigner (for example, a French national), they will need cantonal authorization. Currently, this transaction does not require additional steps, but under the new rules, the process may become longer and more complex.

💡 Impact on the Ticino Real Estate Market
> Ticino, with its hubs like Lugano, Locarno, and Bellinzona, is one of Switzerland’s most sought-after regions for foreign real estate purchases, particularly by Italians and Germans. The new rules could reduce demand for second homes and real estate investments, leading to a stabilization or drop in prices in some areas. However, the government hopes this measure will free up residential properties for local residents, easing pressure on the rental market.

⚠️ Procedures and Required Documents
> Although the text is still under consultation, we can anticipate the steps applicants will need to follow:
> - For purchasing a primary residence: Submit an authorization request to the relevant cantonal office (in Ticino, likely the Department of Territory and Environment or the municipal office in Lugano/Bellinzona).
> - For selling holiday homes between foreigners: Request cantonal authorization, with a case-by-case evaluation.
> - For commercial properties: Demonstrate that the purchase is intended for personal or business use, not for investment.

### Comparison with Other Cantons
> Ticino is not the only canton affected by these measures. The Canton of Bern has also initiated similar discussions, though with different timelines and modalities. In general, alpine cantons (Ticino, Grisons, Valais) are the most exposed to foreign real estate purchases due to their appeal as tourist destinations and proximity to neighboring countries like Italy, Germany, and France.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). If the new rules come into force, cross-border workers and foreigners intending to purchase property in Ticino will need to comply with a series of stricter procedures. Below are the concrete steps to follow, based on the information currently available.

### Deadlines and timelines
The **public consultation** will remain open until 15 July 2026. During this period, citizens, associations and interested parties can submit observations and proposals for amendments to the Federal Council. Only after the consultation closes will the definitive text of the law be known.

Hypothetical timeline:
- 15 July 2026: Consultation closes.
- End of 2026: Federal Council approves the definitive law.
- 2027: New rules come into force (exact date not yet specified).

### How to prepare for buying property in Ticino
1. Assess your situation
 - If you are an EU/EFTA citizen, the new rules will not directly affect you, as the Lex Koller only applies to third countries.
 - If you are a non-EU citizen, you will need to obtain authorisation to purchase a primary residence or holiday home. Make sure you have all the necessary documentation, such as a Swiss employment contract or proof of residence.

2. Contact the relevant offices
 In Ticino, the responsible office will likely be the Department of Territory and Environment of the Canton of Ticino or your local municipality (e.g., Lugano, Bellinzona or Locarno). Here you can obtain up-to-date information on procedures and required documents.

3. Consider alternatives to real estate investments
 If the new rules prevent you from purchasing investment properties, you might consider other options, such as:
 - Buying property in neighbouring countries (e.g., Italy or France), where restrictions are less stringent.
 - Investing in Swiss real estate funds listed on the stock exchange, although the government plans to ban this option for foreigners.

4. Monitor regulatory developments
 Since the text is still being finalised, it is advisable to stay informed through official channels of the Canton of Ticino, the Confederation or cross-border worker associations such as FACT – Federazione Associazioni Ticinesi dei Frontalieri.

### Useful tools for cross-border workers and investors
To assess the feasibility of a property purchase in Ticino in light of the new rules, you can use:
- [cost-of-living calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to compare expenses between Ticino and Italy.
- [interactive map of Ticino municipalities](nav:border-map) to identify areas with greater property availability.
- [cross-border salary comparison](nav:salary-compare) to check the financial sustainability of a purchase.

### What happens if you do not comply with the new regulations?
The penalties for illegal property purchases in Switzerland by foreigners are already set out in the current Lex Koller and could be tightened. In case of non-compliance, the property could be expropriated or the owner could face financial penalties. It is therefore essential to wait for the definitive text of the law and comply with the indicated procedures.

### In summary: what to do now
- Wait for the definitive text of the law, expected by the end of 2026.
- Consider alternatives if you are a non-EU investor.
- Prepare the necessary documentation for any future authorisations.
- Consult cantonal offices for clarification on procedures.

📌 Warning: The new rules could come into force in 2027. If you are considering buying property in Ticino, carefully evaluate the timing and procedures, especially if you are a non-EU citizen.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/il-governo-svizzero-vuole-limitare-gli-acquisti-immobiliari-dallestero/91260589)*

item.title: Outdoor fires in Ticino: Ban lifted from 1 May 2024
item.description: From 1 May 2024, burning fires in Ticino’s forests and meadows is again permitted, though not in all areas of the nearby Italian-speaking Grisons. Here’s what changes for cross-border workers and the rules to follow.
item.content:encoded:
The Ticino Cantonal Government has lifted the ban on lighting outdoor fires in forests, meadows, and unprotected areas of the Canton of Ticino. The decision came into effect **on 1 May 2024**, following months of restrictions due to the risk of summer wildfires. However, the new regulation does not apply to the nearby Italian-speaking Grisons, where the ban remains in place at least until the next evaluation by local authorities.

The lifting of the ban was justified by improved weather conditions and a reduced wildfire risk, according to the Ticino Department of Territory. The updated rules apply to all areas not classified as high-risk zones, where the fire danger is still considered significant. Cantonal authorities have clarified that local permits may still impose additional restrictions in the event of adverse weather conditions.

### Background of the measure
The outdoor fire ban was introduced in 2023 following an exceptionally dry summer and several deliberate and accidental fires, particularly affecting the Sopraceneri region. The measure caused inconvenience, especially for those who, during the winter and spring months, were used to spending Sundays outdoors lighting small fires for barbecues or warming drinks. The lifting of the ban had already been anticipated by several Ticino municipalities starting in autumn 2023, but it was only with the arrival of spring 2024 that the Cantonal Government officially confirmed the decision.

### Differences with the Italian-speaking Grisons
The situation remains unchanged in the nearby Italian-speaking Grisons, where the ban on lighting outdoor fires is still in force. This discrepancy creates significant challenges for cross-border workers living in border areas between Ticino and the Grisons, such as Brusio or Poschiavo. In these zones, Ticino residents can now light fires, while their Italian neighbours cannot. Ticino cantonal authorities have emphasised that Swiss regulations are more lenient than Italian ones, which maintain stricter restrictions even in less critical months.

> «The lifting of the ban responds to a technical assessment based on weather data and ground moisture levels. However, each municipality can decide to maintain local restrictions if it deems necessary.» — Ticino Department of Territory

📊 Impact on outdoor activities
The possibility of lighting outdoor fires primarily affects those who engage in hiking, camping, or simple Sunday barbecues in wooded areas. According to data from the Ticino Tourism Federation, the lifting of the ban is expected to boost tourism in the spring months, which are traditionally less popular than summer. However, authorities have reiterated that the rules do not authorise lighting fires in protected areas or near sensitive infrastructure, such as power lines or fuel depots.

The updated regulations also apply to fires made with locally collected wood but prohibit the use of petrol or other chemical accelerants, considered too dangerous.

[object Object] The lifting of the ban on lighting open-air fires in Ticino marks a significant change for residents and visitors to the canton’s wooded areas. However, for cross-border workers who regularly travel between Ticino and the Italian-speaking Grisons, the new regulations introduce a series of **practical and administrative complications** that deserve close attention.

### Before vs. after: what has changed for cross-border workers
Until 30 April 2024, lighting open-air fires in Ticino was prohibited, except in expressly authorised areas and under strict supervision. The lifting of the ban now allows people to resume traditional activities such as barbecues in the woods, warming themselves by a campfire, or simply enjoying the outdoors. However, for those living in areas bordering the Italian-speaking Grisons, the situation is more complex. In Brusio and Poschiavo, for example, residents are permitted to light fires, while those living in nearby Tirano are still subject to Italy’s ban. This disparity can lead to paradoxical situations: a cross-border worker living in Brusio and working in Italy could light a fire in their own garden, but could not do so just a few hundred metres away in the town of residence.

💡 Key insight: The new rules apply based on the location where the fire is lit, not where the person resides or works. This means that even a cross-border worker subject to Italian law at home must comply with Ticino’s regulations when lighting a fire in Ticino.

### Risks and responsibilities: what the law says
Under Ticino’s new regulations, the person who lights and manages the fire bears sole responsibility. In the event of an accidental or deliberate fire, cantonal authorities may hold the individual liable for any environmental or material damage. As previously mentioned, fines for serious violations can reach up to 10,000 Swiss francs (CHF).

For cross-border workers moving between the two cantons, it is essential to familiarise themselves with the rules of the municipality where the fire is lit, not just where they live or work. For example:
- If a fire is lit in Brusio (Ticino), Ticino’s regulations apply.
- If the same campfire is lit in nearby Tirano (Italian-speaking Grisons), Italy’s still-active ban prohibits the action.

This scenario can create conflicts of jurisdiction between local authorities, particularly in cases where fires spread across cantonal borders. Ticino authorities have already announced that, in an emergency, they will intervene immediately to extinguish flames, but civil liability will remain with the person who lit the fire, regardless of nationality.

⚠️ Important: Even if a fire is lit in compliance with Ticino’s rules, cross-border workers must ensure it does not violate Italian regulations if the fire spreads or if they are in Italian territory.

### Impact on tourism and recreational activities
The lifting of the ban could boost local tourism, especially in May and June, traditionally quieter months compared to summer. According to data from the Ticino Tourism Federation, wooded areas in both the Sopraceneri and Sottoceneri regions may see an increase in visitors, thanks to the ability to safely light fires.

However, cantonal authorities have emphasised that the new rules do not permit fires in protected areas, such as natural parks or forest reserves. Additionally, the use of flammable materials or large-scale fires is strictly prohibited, as these could pose a danger to the surrounding vegetation.

📊 Data point: The Ticino Tourism Federation estimates that the lifting of the ban could lead to a 15-20% increase in visits to wooded areas during the spring months, provided weather conditions remain favourable.

> As stated by the Ticino cantonal authorities: 'While we encourage responsible enjoyment of our natural spaces, safety and respect for the environment remain our top priorities.'

For hikers and campers, the lifting of the ban comes as a relief, but the authorities have reiterated the need to follow basic rules:

1. Always check local weather conditions before lighting a fire.
2. Maintain a minimum distance of 10 metres from buildings, forests, and roads.
3. Never leave a fire unattended.
4. Fully extinguish all embers before leaving the site.

### What happens in the event of an accidental fire
In the event of an accidental fire caused by improperly managed fires, the Ticino cantonal authorities have the right to demand compensation for damages from the person who lit the fire. This also applies to cross-border workers living in Italy but engaging in recreational activities in Ticino. Compensation procedures can be initiated directly by the Cantonale Forestale (Ticino Forestry Service) or local police forces.

According to data from Ticino’s Civil Protection, 12 forest fires were recorded in 2023 due to poorly managed fires, with estimated damages exceeding CHF 500,000. The lifting of the ban does not reduce the authorities’ vigilance, which will continue to monitor high-risk areas closely.

📊 Cultural differences and local customs
In Ticino, lighting outdoor fires is a common practice, especially during holidays such as Swiss National Day (1 August) or spring hiking trips. In the Italian-speaking Grisons, however, restrictions are stricter and often tied to stricter environmental regulations aimed at protecting historic wooded areas.

These cultural differences can lead to misunderstandings among cross-border workers, particularly those living near the border. For example, a cross-border worker who lights a fire in Brusio without realising it is prohibited in Tirano could face civil penalties from Italian authorities, in addition to Swiss penalties if the fire spreads across the border.

### Tips for cross-border workers
To avoid unpleasant surprises, cross-border workers living or passing through border areas are advised to:
- Check local rules on outdoor fires with their municipality of residence.
- Monitor weather conditions before lighting any fire, using bulletins from MeteoSwiss or the Regional Agency for Environmental Protection (ARPA) in Lombardy for Italian areas.
- Use only locally collected wood and avoid chemical accelerants, which are banned in both cantons.
- Keep a fire extinguisher or bucket of water handy to quickly douse any uncontrolled flames.
- Report any fires or dangerous behaviour to the relevant authorities using 118 (emergencies in Italy) or 117 (emergencies in Switzerland).

### Future outlook
Ticino authorities have announced that the lifting of the ban will be reviewed monthly based on evolving weather conditions. If the risk of fire increases, the ban could be reintroduced even before the end of September 2024. For this reason, it is advisable to constantly monitor updates on the Ticino Department of Territory website or official Civil Protection channels.

💡 Key takeaways for cross-border workers
- Respect local fire regulations in both Switzerland and Italy to avoid fines.
- Always prioritise safety and environmental protection.
- Stay informed about changing rules to prevent accidents and legal consequences.

⚠️ Need help or have questions?
Contact the Ticino Cantonale Forestale or your local Italian authorities for guidance on fire regulations in your area.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). The lifting of the ban on lighting outdoor fires in Ticino opens up new recreational opportunities, but it also requires a **thorough understanding of the rules** and careful management of recreational activities. For cross-border workers living or working in border areas, the regulations introduce additional complexities due to differences between Ticino's laws and those of the Italian-speaking Grisons. Below is a step-by-step guide to safely lighting a fire while complying with the regulations, along with a checklist to avoid penalties.

### Step 1: Check local conditions
Before lighting a fire, it is essential to check the weather conditions and the fire risk level in your area. The Ticino authorities update the fire risk daily on the [Department of Territory](nav:whatif) website. For cross-border workers in areas along the border with the Italian-speaking Grisons, it is also advisable to consult bulletins from the Italian Civil Protection or ARPA Lombardia.

### Step 2: Choose a suitable location
Not all locations are suitable for lighting a fire. According to the new Ticino regulations, it is prohibited to light fires in the following areas:
- Protected areas (natural parks, forest reserves).
- Near buildings, roads, power lines, or fuel storage facilities.
- Dry terrain or forests, where the risk of fire spread is high.
- Locations where the fire could pose a public safety hazard.

Ticino municipalities may impose additional local restrictions. For example, some municipalities may ban fires in non-protected areas during periods of extreme drought. To verify, you can contact your local municipality or consult the Department of Territory website.

### Step 3: Gather the right materials
The wood used must be dry and collected on-site. It is prohibited to use:
- Chemically treated lumber.
- Synthetic or flammable materials (plastic, paper, cardboard).
- Petrol, alcohol, or other chemical accelerants.

The embers must be completely extinguished before leaving the site. An effective method for extinguishing the fire is to cover it with soil and pour plenty of water until the embers are completely cool.

### Step 4: Light and monitor the fire
Once lit, the fire must be constantly monitored and kept at a maximum height of 50 cm. It is prohibited to leave it unattended, even for short periods. Authorities recommend:
- Maintaining a minimum distance of 10 meters from buildings, roads, and wooded areas.
- Not exceeding the permitted size (maximum height 50 cm, maximum diameter 1 metre).
- Completely extinguishing the fire after use, ensuring no embers remain lit.

### Step 5: Extinguish and clean up
After use, it is mandatory to fully extinguish the fire and collect any waste. An effective method to verify the fire is out includes:
- Pouring water on the embers until they stop smoking.
- Stirring the embers with soil to accelerate cooling.
- Ensuring the surrounding ground is not hot to the touch.

Leaving waste or lit embers can result in administrative penalties, and in the event of an accidental fire, civil and criminal liability.

> 📊💡⚠️ Checklist for lighting a fire in Ticino (2024)
> - [ ] Check the fire risk level and weather conditions.
> - [ ] Verify local restrictions with your municipality or the Department of Territory.
> - [ ] Choose a location at least 10 metres from buildings, roads, and forests.
> - [ ] Use only dry, untreated wood collected on-site.
> - [ ] Keep the fire height under 50 cm and diameter under 1 metre.
> - [ ] Never leave the fire unattended.
> - [ ] Fully extinguish the fire with water and soil before leaving.
> - [ ] Collect all waste and ensure no embers remain lit.

[object Object]

### ⚠️ Final warning

The revocation of the ban is valid only until 30 September 2024 and may be revoked at any time if the fire risk increases. Keep an eye on official updates and, if in doubt, refrain from lighting fires until conditions are deemed safe again.

Evaluating a Ticino job offer? Simulate your [net payslip](nav:payslip): enter gross salary, marital status and municipality for a detailed breakdown.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/fuochi-allaperto-divieto-revocato-in-ticino-ma-non-nel-grigioni-italiano/)*

item.title: Oil and Gas in Switzerland: Secure Supply by 2026
item.description: Despite tensions in the Middle East, Switzerland guarantees stable supplies of oil and gas. Here’s where they come from and the impact on prices for cross-border workers and Ticinesi.
item.content:encoded:
On March 25, 2026, as the conflict between Israel, the United States, and Iran intensified, gasoline and diesel prices in Switzerland surged. Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, blocking roughly one-fifth of global crude oil trade. While Switzerland does not rely directly on Persian Gulf oil, global energy market fluctuations were felt across the country.

According to Avenergy, the organization representing Switzerland’s petroleum market stakeholders, the supply situation remains under control. On March 9, 2026, spokesperson Ueli Bamert stated: >Our supply of petroleum products is always guaranteed; there is no fear of shortages.> The resilience of logistics and the ability to import fuels from other regions have prevented disruptions.

The Steering Committee for Energy Supply Security, which includes political, business, and cantonal representatives, confirmed on March 17, 2026, that there are no immediate risks to Switzerland’s energy supply. However, tensions in the Middle East could keep prices unstable for months, with repercussions for Switzerland’s—and Ticino’s—economy.

📊 **Switzerland’s supply routes**

Switzerland imports all the fossil fuels it consumes, as it has no domestic resources. In 2025, the country purchased 1.5 million tons of oil from the United States, worth 691 million francs. The U.S. has been Switzerland’s top crude supplier since 2023, following the lifting of the 1955 export ban and advancements in extraction techniques like fracking. Nigeria ranks as the second-largest supplier, followed by countries outside the Middle East.

Petroleum-derived products account for nearly half of Switzerland’s energy needs (46% in 2024), while natural gas makes up about 12%, primarily used for heating and cooking. This diversified supply mix reduces dependence on the Persian Gulf but does not eliminate global price fluctuations.

⚠️ Impact on prices for cross-border workers and Ticinesi

Between February 28 and March 9, 2026, diesel prices rose by 8.6%, while heating oil surged by 40%. Despite stable supplies, cross-border workers and Ticinesi residents are feeling the indirect effects of the crisis. Transport and heating costs could rise further if the conflict persists, affecting household and business budgets.

💡 Comparison with the pre-2023 situation

Before 2023, Libya was Switzerland’s top oil supplier. Its replacement by the U.S. has made the country less exposed to Middle Eastern instability, though not immune to global price swings. Switzerland spends an average of eight billion francs annually on oil, gas, and nuclear fuels, according to the Swiss Energy Foundation.

Despite a secure supply, cross-border workers commuting from Lombardy to Ticino may face higher pump prices and heating costs, directly impacting their cost of living. The tensions in the Middle East are reshaping energy supply dynamics not only for Switzerland but also for cross-border workers who commute daily between Ticino and Lombardy. Reliance on non-European suppliers such as the USA or Nigeria exposes commuters to price fluctuations that directly impact household budgets.

### Possible scenarios for cross-border workers and residents

If the conflict were to persist for months, gasoline and diesel prices could see further increases. Analysts warn that the energy market will remain unstable even after normal production activities resume in the Middle East. For commuters traveling daily on the A2 or A9 motorways, this means higher transport costs: a worker living in Varese and working in Lugano could spend up to 200 francs more per month just on fuel, assuming an average monthly distance of 1,500 km.

Heating costs could also rise. Natural gas, which meets 12% of Switzerland’s energy needs, is subject to international volatility. For a Ticinese family living in a hilly area like Bellinzona or Locarno, the 40% increase in heating oil prices recorded between February and March 2026 translates into an additional monthly expense of at least 200–300 francs for heating.

📊 Key takeaways:
- Cross-border workers face higher transport and heating costs due to Middle East tensions.
- Swiss energy supply remains stable, but prices are volatile.
- Italian side (Lombardy) may see sharper price hikes than Ticino.

### Differences between Ticino and Lombardy

While Switzerland ensures stable energy supplies, Lombardy relies more heavily on imports from the Middle East. This means commuters living in Italy could be more exposed to price hikes. For example, gasoline prices in Como or Varese could rise more sharply than in Lugano or Chiasso, creating a cost gap between the two sides of the border.

💡 Tip: Cross-border workers can compare fuel prices in real time using apps like [car-cost](nav:car-cost), which tracks stations near crossings such as Brogeda or Gaggiolo.

### Saving opportunities

Cross-border workers can reduce the impact of price hikes by adopting practical strategies:

- Fuel: Take advantage of price differences between Swiss and Italian service stations. Apps like [car-cost](nav:car-cost) allow real-time comparisons between border crossings.
- Heating: For those using heating oil, consider purchasing fuel in advance during periods of lower prices. Ticinese consumer cooperatives often offer collective discounts on heating oil purchases.
- Public transport: In Ticino, public transport options such as SBB trains or regional buses could be a cost-effective alternative, especially for those living near stations like Mendrisio or Bellinzona.

⚠️ Warning: While Switzerland’s energy supply is secure, prices remain volatile. Cross-border workers should budget for potential increases in transport and heating costs.

### The role of institutions

The Steering Committee for Energy Supply Security has reiterated that there are no immediate risks to Switzerland’s energy supply, but emphasized the need for close monitoring. For cross-border workers, this means supplies will be guaranteed, but prices may fluctuate. Ticino’s cantonal authorities, such as the Department of Territory and Environment, are evaluating support measures for the most affected households, though no specific initiatives for cross-border workers have been announced yet.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). With rising oil and gas prices, cross-border workers and Ticino residents need to adapt their habits to manage costs. Here’s a step-by-step guide to handling the situation effectively, based on guidance from Swiss authorities and sector organizations.

### Step 1: Monitor fuel prices

To save on gasoline and diesel, it’s essential to compare prices between Swiss and Italian service stations. In Ticino, prices can vary by up to 10% between locations. Tools like [car-cost](nav:car-cost) provide real-time comparisons of:

- Service stations in Chiasso, Mendrisio, or Stabio
- Outlets in the provinces of Como or Varese

**Practical tip**: Refuel in Ticino if the price is at least 0.10 CHF/litre lower than in Lombardy. For those crossing the border daily, this can translate to savings of over 100 francs per month.

### Step 2: Explore heating alternatives

If you use heating oil or natural gas, check whether you can purchase supplies during periods of lower prices. Ticino’s consumer cooperatives, such as those in Lugano or Bellinzona, often offer discounted rates for bulk purchases. For those who can, consider switching to heat pump or wood-burning systems, which are less exposed to energy market fluctuations.

⚠️ Important: Before stockpiling fuel, verify the cantonal regulations on fuel storage. In Ticino, storage is regulated by the Department of Territory and Environment.

### Step 3: Optimize travel

For cross-border workers using a car, consider alternative routes or public transport. Ticino’s SBB and regional bus services offer efficient connections between cities like Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno. For example, the train from Como to Lugano takes about 40 minutes and costs less than fuel for the same trip.

📊 Useful tool: The [cost-of-living calculator](nav:cost-of-living) lets you compare transport expenses between private cars and public transport, including tolls and insurance.

### Step 4: Apply for subsidies

While no specific measures have been announced for cross-border workers, Ticino residents can check for heating or transport subsidies. The canton has set up a solidarity fund for economically vulnerable families, but applications must be submitted within the specified deadlines.

Useful contacts:
- Ticino Cantonal Energy Office: for information on energy efficiency incentives
- Municipal utilities in Lugano, Bellinzona, or Locarno: to check for discounts on gas and electricity

### Step 5: Plan ahead

Given the uncertainty over future prices, it’s wise to plan energy expenses for the coming months. If you anticipate needing to buy fuel or heating oil, purchasing in advance during lower-price periods can mitigate the impact of price hikes.

⚠️ Warning: Avoid excessive stockpiling, especially of heating oil, which can degrade over time. Always check local regulations on safe fuel storage.

### What if the situation worsens?

If the conflict persists or global supply disruptions continue, Swiss authorities may activate emergency plans. However, the Energy Supply Steering Committee has assured there are no immediate risks of shortages. Cross-border workers are advised to monitor official updates via Ticino’s channels and sector organizations like Avenergy.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/lavoro-ed-economia/petrolio-e-gas-in-svizzera-approvvigionamento-sicuro-nonostante-il-conflitto-in-medio-oriente/91143350)*

item.title: SlowUp 2026: Road Closures and Limited Transport Between Bellinzona and Locarno
item.description: On Sunday, 19 April 2026, five districts in Bellinzona will close their streets for SlowUp. Public transport will be disrupted: lines 3, 62.213 and 62.311 will face cancellations and detours between 7:00 and 18:30.
item.content:encoded:
The Ticino Cantonal Police have announced traffic restrictions for the **SlowUp 2026** event, scheduled for Sunday, April 19. Road access between Bellinzona and Locarno will be partially closed and subject to time-based limitations. Traffic will be regulated between 9:00 AM and 5:00 PM, with transit banned on 12 roads in the cantonal capital.

### Roads affected in Bellinzona
In the central district, the following streets will be closed: via M. Comacini, via Raggi, via Chicherio, viale Franscini, via Salvioni, via Orico, piazza Governo, via Dogana, via Ghiringhelli, via Torti, via Convento and via Saleggi. San Giovanni Hospital will remain accessible via viale Franscini.

In Camorino, closures will include via In Arla (from the intersection with via al Mai), via al Mai, via al Guast, I Casasc, In Cumlina and Al Lischee. In Giubiasco, Contrada Gada will remain open in both directions, while Cima Piazza will only be accessible via via Corgella and Contrada Gada. Via Ronchetti will only be reachable via Contrada Gada, with guaranteed access to and from via Ponte Vecchio, via E. Berta and via Borghetto up to the intersection with via Pedevilla. Via Fossato will also remain closed, except for access from the nursing home to public parking areas.

Residents of via Franscini, Bonasco and Pedevilla will have access via the intersection of via C. Olgiati and via Saleggi/via F. Zorzi, near the Tre Castelli restaurant.

### Restrictions in Gudo and surrounding areas
In Gudo, the following roads will be closed: via al Piano, via Stradonino (Cadenazzo-Gudo section), via Cantonale (from the intersection with via Stradonino), via al Gaggiolo Lungo, via Boscerine and via al Gaggiolo (from strada della Pobbia). Residents of Progero, on the left bank of the cantonal road, will only be able to cross in the Progero area to reach the hillside roads. Inhabitants of Al Bosco, Malacarne and Caneggio will be able to travel freely between Bellinzona and Locarno without restrictions.

### How to prepare
The Police urge residents planning to use motor vehicles during the event to park the day before outside the official route, as roads will be closed from 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM. Traffic will be managed by auxiliary personnel and temporary signage. No exceptions will be made for private vehicles, except for residents of the affected areas, who will have access via alternative routes already communicated. The restrictions introduced by SlowUp 2026 will have a direct impact on cross-border workers and regular commuters traveling between Bellinzona, Locarno, and the surrounding areas. Changes to public transport, in particular, could force people to alter long-standing habits, especially those who commute for work or study.

### Public transport: changes to routes
AutoPostale Svizzera Regione Ticino has announced the following adjustments for Sunday, 19 April:

- **Line 3 (Bellinzona - Sant’Antonino)**: the section between Bellinzona Station and Sant’Antonino Shopping Centres will be suspended from 7:00 to 18:30. Passengers will need to alight at Bellinzona Station or Sant’Antonino Village.
- Line 62.213 (Sant’Antonino - Vigana - Giubiasco Station): between 9:00 and 18:00, the Sant’Antonino terminus will be at Sant’Antonino Village, with the entire section between Sant’Antonino Station and Village closed due to the closure of via Essagra.
- Line 62.311 (Bellinzona - Locarno): between 9:30 and 17:00, there will be no service between Gudo Chiesa and Riazzino Station. The route will be split into two sections: Bellinzona Station - Gudo Chiesa - Bellinzona Station and Locarno Roundabout - Riazzino Station - Locarno Roundabout.

### Possible scenarios for commuters
Those who regularly use these routes will need to plan alternative paths. For example, to reach Sant’Antonino from Bellinzona Station, passengers will have to alight at Bellinzona Station and continue on foot or by other means, as the direct service will be suspended. Similarly, those traveling from Locarno to Gudo will need to account for the split in the route of line 62.311, requiring a change of transport at Bellinzona Station or Locarno Roundabout.

For cross-border workers who live in Lombardy but work in Ticino, the situation could prove even more challenging. If you usually commute by car to work, it will be essential to check whether your route is affected by closures and plan ahead with earlier departures or alternative solutions, such as carpooling or cycling.

📊 Impact on cross-border workers
> The closures may lead to longer travel times or the need to switch to different modes of transport, increasing the complexity of daily commutes.

### Comparison with previous editions
In previous editions of SlowUp, road closures were primarily concentrated on secondary routes, whereas in 2026 the impact will also affect key connections between towns and cities. This could result in greater inconvenience for those who commute daily for work or study, forcing them to take longer routes or consider alternative transport options.

💡 Tips for commuters
- Check updated timetables on the AutoPostale website before traveling.
- Consider combining different transport modes to minimize disruptions.
- If you hold a cross-border worker permit (G permit), verify whether your route is affected by closures and plan accordingly.

⚠️ Important note
> Always allow extra travel time during SlowUp 2026, as delays and diversions are likely on affected routes.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). For anyone traveling between Bellinzona, Locarno and the surrounding areas during SlowUp 2026, it is essential to plan your journey in advance to avoid disruptions. Here is a practical guide to help you get organized.

### Steps for residents and cross-border workers
1. **Check your usual route**: Verify whether your regular commuting path is among the closed roads. The full list is available on the website of the Ticino Cantonal Police or the official SlowUp Ticino portal.

2. Park outside the restricted area: If you are driving, park your car on Saturday, April 18 in a zone unaffected by closures, such as the exchange parking lots in Bellinzona or Locarno. Avoid using your vehicle during the event.

3. Use public transport with care: If you rely on modified routes (3, 62.213, 62.311), check the updated timetables on the [official AutoPostale Ticino website](nav:transport). Consider traveling before or after the closure period (9 AM–5 PM).

4. Alternative routes: Residents of Giubiasco or Camorino may want to reach Bellinzona station on foot or by bike, as access roads could be congested.

5. Access to San Giovanni Hospital: Patients and visitors can still reach the hospital via viale Franscini, which will remain open for emergencies and essential services.

### What to do in an emergency
